note_data.title: Introduction to organic chemistry
keyPoints: **Organic compounds** are compounds that contain carbon atoms bonded to hydrogen atoms
**Structural formula** shows the arrangement of atoms in a molecule and how they are bonded
**Homologous series** is a family of organic compounds with the same functional group and general formula
Members of a homologous series differ by **CH₂** and have similar chemical properties but different physical properties
**Isomers** are compounds with the same molecular formula but different structural formulae
**Functional group** is the part of an organic molecule that determines its chemical properties
Carbon atoms can form **four covalent bonds** and can bond to other carbon atoms to form chains
Physical properties in a homologous series show **gradual change** as the carbon chain length increases

definitions.definition: A compound that contains carbon atoms bonded to hydrogen atoms
A family of organic compounds with the same functional group and general formula
A formula that shows the arrangement of atoms in a molecule and how they are bonded
Compounds with the same molecular formula but different structural formulae
The part of an organic molecule that determines its chemical properties

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Show all bonds clearly. Use lines for covalent bonds. Label atoms.

modelAnswer.question: Ethane and butane are members of the alkane homologous series. (a) State two characteristics of a homologous series. (b) Draw the structural formula of butane.
modelAnswer.answer: (a) Same functional group / same general formula / differ by CH₂ / similar chemical properties / gradual change in physical properties (any 2) (b) H-C-C-C-C-H with all hydrogen atoms shown bonded to carbon atoms
markBreakdown: Mark 1: First characteristic of homologous series
Mark 2: Second characteristic of homologous series
Mark 3: Correct carbon chain for butane
Mark 4: All hydrogen atoms shown correctly bonded

commonMistakes: Never say organic compounds 'contain carbon' - always say 'contain carbon bonded to hydrogen'
Students often forget to show ALL hydrogen atoms in structural formulae
Drawing condensed formulae when structural formulae are asked for - show all bonds as lines
Confusing molecular formula with structural formula - structural shows arrangement of atoms
Saying isomers have 'different formulas' when they have the same molecular formula but different structural formulae
examinerTips: This topic appears in every paper - know definitions word-perfect
Always count carbon and hydrogen atoms carefully when drawing structural formulae
Practice drawing isomers - this is a common 4-6 mark question
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Structural formulae showing all atoms and bonds as lines, e.g., methane with C in center bonded to 4 H atoms, ethane showing C-C bond with hydrogens attached
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q1 (3 marks) — they asked students to define organic chemistry and give two examples of organic compounds found in everyday life
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P1 Q4 (2 marks) — students had to identify which compounds from a list were organic and explain their reasoning
This topic was examined in M/J 2024 P2 Q2a (4 marks) — candidates were required to explain why carbon forms so many compounds and describe two key properties of organic compounds
This topic featured in O/N 2023 P1 Q6 (3 marks) — students needed to distinguish between organic and inorganic compounds using given molecular formulas

workedExample.question: Explain why carbon is able to form such a large number of organic compounds. Give two examples of organic compounds and state one use for each. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Explain carbon's ability - Carbon has 4 valence electrons, allowing it to form 4 covalent bonds
Step 2: Mention bonding versatility - Carbon can bond to other carbon atoms forming chains, rings, and branched structures
Step 3: State catenation - This property is called catenation - the ability to form long chains
Step 4: Give first example with use - Example 1: Methane (CH₄) - used as natural gas for heating and cooking
Step 5: Give second example with use - Example 2: Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) - used in alcoholic beverages and as a solvent
workedExample.answer: Carbon has 4 valence electrons in its outer shell, enabling it to form 4 covalent bonds with other atoms. Carbon atoms can bond with other carbon atoms to form long chains, branched structures, and rings through a property called catenation. This allows for an enormous variety of structures. Examples: (1) Methane (CH₄) - used as natural gas for heating and cooking (2) Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) - used in alcoholic beverages and as a solvent.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for mentioning 4 valence electrons/4 bonds, 1 mark for catenation/chain formation, 1 mark for each organic compound correctly named, 1 mark for each correct use stated

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused organic compounds with compounds containing oxygen' — always remember that organic compounds contain carbon-hydrogen bonds, not just carbon

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently gave inorganic compounds as examples when asked for organic compounds' — avoid compounds like CO₂, CaCO₃, and NaHCO₃ which are inorganic despite containing carbon

Examiner Report 2024: 'Candidates often failed to explain catenation clearly' — always define catenation as the ability of carbon atoms to bond with other carbon atoms to form chains, not just 'carbon bonds to itself'
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw the electron arrangement of carbon showing 4 electrons in the outer shell (2,4), and simple structural formulas showing carbon chains. Key diagram: Draw methane (CH₄) showing carbon in center with 4 hydrogen atoms arranged tetrahedrally, and ethane (C₂H₆) showing two carbon atoms bonded together with hydrogen atoms. Include dot-and-cross diagrams showing shared electron pairs in covalent bonds.
note_data.quickRecap: Organic chemistry studies carbon-containing compounds (except CO₂, carbonates, etc.). Carbon has 4 valence electrons allowing 4 covalent bonds and catenation (chain formation). This creates millions of possible structures from simple alkanes to complex proteins. Examples include methane (fuel), ethanol (alcohol), and glucose (energy source).
